comprehensive_summary:
Ovid (Publius Ovidius Naso) is a Roman poet most frequently clued through his epic poem the Metamorphoses, his elegiac love poetry collection Amores, and his didactic poem Ars Amatoria. The Metamorphoses, a fifteen-book narrative poem recounting mythological transformations, is by far his most clued work. It opens with the creation of the universe and the four (or five) ages of man, paralleling Hesiod's account. Key stories within the Metamorphoses include the tragedy of Pyramus and Thisbe (a man stabs himself after falsely believing a lion ate his beloved, causing mulberries to turn purple; the lovers communicated through a crack in their wall), the tale of Pomona and Vertumnus (including the disguised old lady telling the story of Iphis hanging himself), the ekphrastic tapestry of Arachne, and the myth of Iphis transformed into a man. The poem's fifteenth and final book centers on apotheosis, discussing Pythagoras and his doctrine of metempsychosis, the soul of Julius Caesar becoming a comet, and the famous closing line 'vivam' ('I will live'). The poem ends with a claim that the poet's fame will live eternally beyond the stars. The Metamorphoses was influenced by an earlier work by Nicander of Colophon. Arthur Golding produced the first complete English translation using 'fourteeners' rather than the original hexameters, a translation Ezra Pound called the most beautiful book in the English language. Ted Hughes wrote Tales from Ovid, a retelling of twenty-four stories from the poem. The medieval Ovide Moralise is a vast verse adaptation with ethical commentary in Old French. Jeffrey Eugenides references the Metamorphoses in Middlesex, where Cal translates 'ex ovo omnia' and markets himself as Hermaphroditus. Ali Smith's Girl Meets Boy reinterprets the myth of Iphis. Patricia Westerford in Richard Powers' The Overstory receives a copy and muses on its opening line.

Ovid's Amores is an elegiac poetry collection in which Cupid steals a metrical foot to force the poet to write elegiac couplets. A poem from the Amores has the speaker succumb to being a 'fresh prize' for a god who 'stole a foot away' and shot him through the heart with an arrow. Donna Zuckerberg's book Not All Dead White Men includes a chapter titled 'The Ovid Method' demanding an ironic reading of his poem calling a mistress' birthday 'one of great terror.' Christopher Marlowe translated an elegy by Ovid, changing the speaker's lament of impotence into insults toward his partner. Geoffrey Hill's poem 'Ovid in the Third Reich' examines Nazi atrocities by juxtaposition with the immorality Ovid advocated in the Amores.

Ovid's Ars Amatoria is a didactic work written in elegiac couplets (unconventionally for didactic poetry) that gives advice on love and seduction. Its first two of three sections give male readers advice on how to pick up women. It humorously likens love to military service and imagines the Rape of the Sabine Women as a theatrical production. The Ars Amatoria inspired Andreas Capellanus's influential 12th-century work on courtly love. It was followed by the Remedia Amoris.

Ovid's exile is a major biographical topic. He was exiled (likely by Augustus) and spent his years of banishment in Tomis near the Black Sea. He attributed his exile to 'carmen et error' ('a poem and a mistake') in his Tristia, with the 'carmen' referring to the Ars Amatoria and the 'error' still fueling speculation, including theories about a scandal involving Augustus's daughters. The Tristia also comments that the exile resulted in the Metamorphoses being 'cut short by its author's sad flight.' His Letters from the Black Sea (Epistulae ex Ponto) were also written in Tomis. David Malouf fictionalized the exile in his novella An Imaginary Life. Ovid also wrote the curse poem Ibis, which may target Augustus, inspired by a poem by Callimachus. Ovid is also noted for having had a large nose, referenced humorously in a Quevedo poem about Gongora.

works.name: Metamorphoses
works.description: The Metamorphoses is Ovid's fifteen-book epic narrative poem recounting mythological transformations, from the creation of the universe through the apotheosis of Julius Caesar. It is the most frequently clued work by Ovid, appearing in multiple tossups and bonus parts. The poem opens with the creation of the world and the ages of man, and its final book discusses Pythagoras, metempsychosis, and Julius Caesar's soul becoming a comet. Key stories include Pyramus and Thisbe, Pomona and Vertumnus, Arachne's tapestry, and Iphis. The poem has been translated by Arthur Golding (in fourteeners), retold by Ted Hughes in Tales from Ovid, and adapted in the medieval Ovide Moralise.

clues.clue: A fifteen-book poem recounting mythological transformations, written by Ovid
quotes: This long narrative poem recounts stories of mythological transformations.
For 10 points, name this text that centers on transformation and was written by Ovid.

clues.clue: The story of Pyramus and Thisbe: a man stabs himself after thinking a lion ate his beloved, turning mulberries purple; they communicated through a crack in their wall
quotes: a man's blood shoots out like a burst pipe after he stabs himself, making the mulberries next to him purple
This text originated a story in which a man dies after thinking that a lion ate his beloved, whom he talked to using a crack between their walls.

clues.clue: The final book (Book 15) discusses Pythagoras, metempsychosis, and ends with the apotheosis of Julius Caesar (his soul becomes a comet/star)
quotes: This text's fifteenth and final book closes with the apotheosis of Julius Caesar.
In its fifteenth book, this work discusses the appearance of a comet that contains the spirit of Julius Caesar.
Name this poem that ends 'vivam,' or 'I will live,' after a paean to Augustus and a description of the soul of a murdered politician becoming a comet.

clues.clue: Opens with creation of the universe, followed by the four (or five) ages of man (paralleling Hesiod)
quotes: It's not by Hesiod, but the first book of this text outlines how mankind became impious during its fourth and final age, the Iron Age.
This work begins with the creation of the universe, followed by the four ages of man.

clues.clue: Arthur Golding produced the first complete English translation using 'fourteeners' rather than the original hexameters
quotes: Arthur Golding used 'fourteeners' rather than the original hexameters in the first complete translation of this work into English.
Ezra Pound's ABCs of Reading claims that Arthur Golding's translation of this book was the most beautiful book in the English language.

clues.clue: Ted Hughes wrote Tales from Ovid, retelling twenty-four stories from the poem
quotes: A retelling of twenty-four stories from this work was written by Ted Hughes and titled for Tales from its author.

clues.clue: The story of Pomona and Vertumnus, including Vertumnus disguised as an old lady telling the story of Iphis hanging himself
quotes: While trying to gain access to an orchard, a man in this text disguised as an old lady tells the story of Iphis hanging himself on his beloved's doorway.

clues.clue: Contains the ekphrastic tapestry of Arachne (in Book 6)
quotes: A detailed ekphrasis on the tapestry woven by Arachne is included in the Metamorphoses.

clues.clue: Personification of Fame (fama) lives in a mountain-top brass house with a thousand doorless entrances (Book 13); 'free' fame prefers Augustus to Julius Caesar
quotes: In Book 13, the personification of this concept lives in a mountain-top brass house with a thousand doorless entrances.

clues.clue: Influenced by an earlier work by Nicander of Colophon; epilogue claims 'the best part of me will be born, immortal, beyond the distant stars'
quotes: This poem was influenced by an earlier work by Nicander of Colophon and includes an epilogue in which its author claims, 'the best part of me will be born, immortal, beyond the distant stars.'

clues.clue: Parodies epic tradition by describing the death of Hercules, then having Alcmena narrate his birth and describe the intercession of Galanthis
quotes: This work parodies epic tradition by describing the death of Hercules, then having Alcmena narrate his birth and describe the intercession of Galanthis.

clues.clue: The Tristia comments that Ovid's exile resulted in the Metamorphoses being 'cut short by its author's sad flight'
quotes: The Tristia comments that Ovid's exile resulted in this poem being 'cut short by its author's sad flight.'

clues.clue: The medieval Ovide Moralise is a 70,000-line Old French verse adaptation with ethical commentary; William Caxton published a prose version in 1480
quotes: This over 70,000-line manuscript in Old French contains ethical commentary as well as an entire verse adaptation of the Metamorphoses.

clues.clue: Referenced in modern novels: Cal in Eugenides' Middlesex translates 'ex ovo omnia' and works as Hermaphroditus; Ali Smith's Girl Meets Boy reinterprets the myth of Iphis; Patricia Westerford in The Overstory receives a copy
quotes: Cal Stephanides translates this work's phrase 'ex ovo omnia' as 'Everything comes out of an egg' during Miss Barrie's Latin class.
Ovid's myth of Iphis, who is transformed into a man, is reinterpreted in the story of the sisters Anthea and Imogen in this author's novel Girl Meets Boy.

clues.clue: Theorists of its 'anti-Augustanism' analyze unflattering mentions of Atreus/Agamemnon and Saturn/Jove father-son pairs
quotes: Theorists of this poem's 'anti-Augustanism' analyze its unflattering mention of the father-and-son pairs of Atreus and Agamemnon and Saturn and Jove.

works.name: Amores
works.description: The Amores is Ovid's collection of elegiac love poetry. Its key clue is the conceit in which Cupid steals a metrical foot from the poet, forcing him to write in elegiac couplets rather than epic hexameters. The collection is also associated with Christopher Marlowe's translation and Donna Zuckerberg's analysis of 'The Ovid Method' in her book Not All Dead White Men. Geoffrey Hill's 'Ovid in the Third Reich' juxtaposes the Amores' immorality with Nazi atrocities.

clues.clue: Cupid steals a metrical foot, forcing Ovid to write elegiac couplets (from Amores 1.1)
quotes: This Roman poet's Amores imagines Cupid stealing a metrical foot to force him to write elegiac couplets.
The speaker of a poem by this author succumbs to being a 'fresh prize' for a god who 'stole a foot away' and shot him through the heart with an arrow.

clues.clue: Christopher Marlowe translated an elegy from the Amores, changing lament of impotence into insults toward his partner
quotes: Christopher Marlowe's translation of an elegy by this author changes some of the speaker's lament of his impotence into insults toward his partner.

clues.clue: Donna Zuckerberg's 'The Ovid Method' chapter demands an ironic reading of his poem calling a mistress' birthday 'one of great terror'
quotes: A Donna Zuckerberg book's third chapter, titled for this author's 'Method,' demands an ironic reading of his poem calling a mistress' birthday 'one of great terror.'

clues.clue: Geoffrey Hill's 'Ovid in the Third Reich' examines Nazi atrocities by juxtaposition with the immorality Ovid advocated in the Amores
quotes: Another purposefully anachronistic Geoffrey Hill poem examines Nazi atrocities by juxtaposition with the immorality that this poet advocated in Amores.

works.name: Ars Amatoria
works.description: The Ars Amatoria (Art of Love) is Ovid's didactic poem written unconventionally in elegiac couplets. It consists of three sections, with the first two giving male readers advice on picking up women. The poem humorously likens love to military service and imagines the Rape of the Sabine Women as a theatrical production. It is identified as the 'carmen' in Ovid's 'carmen et error' explanation for his exile, and it inspired Andreas Capellanus's medieval work on courtly love.

clues.clue: A didactic work in elegiac couplets whose first two of three sections advise men on picking up women
quotes: This poet unconventionally stuck to elegiac couplets for a didactic work that imagines the Rape of the Sabine Women as a theatrical production.
The first two of the three sections in that poetic work by this author give male readers advice on how to pick up women.
This instructional set of elegies by Ovid, which humorously likens its central subject to military service.

clues.clue: Imagines the Rape of the Sabine Women as a theatrical production
quotes: This poet unconventionally stuck to elegiac couplets for a didactic work that imagines the Rape of the Sabine Women as a theatrical production.

clues.clue: Inspired Andreas Capellanus's 12th-century work on courtly love
quotes: An influential 12th-century work by Andreas Capellanus titled for this concept was inspired by a poetic 'textbook' by Ovid.

clues.clue: Followed by the Remedia Amoris
quotes: It was followed shortly by his Remedia Amoris.

clues.clue: Identified as the 'carmen' in Ovid's 'carmen et error' explanation for his exile
quotes: This phrase first refers to the Ars Amatoria, but its last word still fuels speculation.
This event may have been a result of the publication of Ars Amatoria.

works.name: Exile and Later Works
works.description: Ovid's exile is a major biographical topic in quizbowl. He was exiled (likely by Augustus) and spent his banishment in Tomis near the Black Sea. He attributed his exile to 'carmen et error' ('a poem and a mistake') in the Tristia, with the 'carmen' referring to the Ars Amatoria and the 'error' remaining the subject of speculation (theories include a scandal involving Augustus's daughters). His exile works include the Tristia, Letters from the Black Sea (Epistulae ex Ponto), and the curse poem Ibis. David Malouf fictionalized the exile in An Imaginary Life.

clues.clue: Exiled to Tomis near the Black Sea; attributed exile to 'carmen et error' ('a poem and a mistake') in the Tristia
quotes: Ovid's Tristia uses this three-word Latin phrase to describe why he was exiled.
This event that a poet attributed to 'the anger of an injured god.'

clues.clue: The Tristia: a poetry collection written during exile that tells a 'little book' to 'go, but without ornament'
quotes: A poetry collection written during this event tells a 'little book' to 'go, but without ornament' and 'enter quietly so my verse won't hurt you.'
This event resulted in that poet's Letters from the Black Sea, written in Tomis, and the collection Tristia.

clues.clue: The 'error' in 'carmen et error' fuels speculation, including theories about a scandal involving Augustus's daughters
quotes: its last word still fuels speculation, with theories including a scandal involving Augustus's daughters.

clues.clue: David Malouf fictionalized the exile in his novella An Imaginary Life
quotes: David Malouf fictionalized this event in his novella An Imaginary Life.

clues.clue: Wrote the curse poem Ibis (which may target Augustus), inspired by Callimachus
quotes: Ovid's curse poem Ibis, which may target Augustus, was written after he was subjected to this punishment.

clues.clue: All writings banned from Roman libraries after exile
quotes: After his exile, all of Ovid's writings were banned from the libraries of Rome.

works.name: Heroides
works.description: The Heroides is a collection of verse epistles written by Ovid from the perspectives of mythological heroines. The most notable clue involves Dido's letter to Aeneas, in which she asks 'What crime have I committed except to have loved?' before her suicide.

clues.clue: Dido writes a letter asking 'Who will give over their fields to be controlled by nobodies?' and 'What crime have I committed except to have loved?' before her suicide
quotes: Prior to this event, its central participant writes a letter asking its recipient 'Who will give over their fields to be controlled by nobodies?' and 'What crime have I committed except to have loved?'.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Ovid is identified as a Roman poet of the Amores and Ars Amatoria, and is the author of the Metamorphoses. He wrote in elegiac couplets and is associated with the Augustan age of Latin poetry. A Quevedo poem about Gongora's nose also compares it to Ovid's nose, 'but nosier,' suggesting Ovid was known for having a large nose.

clues.clue: Roman poet of Amores and Ars Amatoria / author of the Metamorphoses
quotes: For 10 points, name this Roman poet of Amores and Ars Amatoria.
Name this Roman poet best known for his Metamorphoses.

clues.clue: Known for having a large nose (referenced in Quevedo's poem about Gongora)
quotes: Quevedo's poem about Gongora's nose also compares it to this author's nose, 'but... nosier.'

Author: the 'carmen' in 'carmen et error' refers to the Ars Amatoria; the 'error' fuels speculation including a scandal involving Augustus's daughters

summary: Ovid (Publius Ovidius Naso) is a Roman poet most frequently clued through his epic poem the Metamorphoses, his elegiac love poetry collection Amores, and his didactic poem Ars Amatoria.
